decision.title: World Bank: Montenegro Now Has Lowest Cross-Border Payment Costs to EU After SEPA Entry
decision.summary: A World Bank report published on 20 February shows Montenegro went from having the highest cross-border payment costs in the Western Balkans in 2025 to the lowest after joining SEPA on 7 October 2025. Transfer costs for €5,000 transactions fell to 0.04-0.05% and for €20,000 transactions to below 0.01%. Transaction speed also improved dramatically — 63% of EU-to-Balkans transfers now complete within 24 hours, up from 6%. Reported by RTNK.
decision.full_text: According to the World Bank's report 'Measuring the Cost of Cross-Border Business-to-Business Payments in the Western Balkans' published in February 2026, Montenegro has achieved a remarkable turnaround. Montenegro shifted from having the highest cross-border payment costs in the Western Balkans in 2025 to having the lowest costs toward the EU after joining SEPA (Single Euro Payments Area) on October 7, 2025. For €5,000 transfers to the EU: costs are approximately 0.04–0.05% of transaction value. For €20,000 transfers to the EU: costs drop below 0.01% of transaction value. Inbound payments from the EU show similarly competitive rates. Transaction processing times improved dramatically: EU to Western Balkans: 6% completed within 24 hours (2024) → 63% (2025); Western Balkans to EU: 39% within 24 hours (2024) → 74% (2025). The Central Bank of Montenegro emphasized these changes demonstrate 'concrete and measurable benefits for the economy' through lower transaction costs and reduced trade barriers with the EU.
decision.title_mne: Svjetska banka: Crna Gora sada ima najniže troškove prekograničnih plaćanja prema EU nakon ulaska u SEPA
decision.summary_mne: Izvještaj Svjetske banke objavljen 20. februara pokazuje da je Crna Gora, nakon pristupanja SEPA-i 7. oktobra 2025. godine, sa pozicije zemlje s najvišim troškovima prekograničnih plaćanja na Zapadnom Balkanu prešla na poziciju zemlje s najnižim troškovima. Troškovi transfera za transakcije od 5.000 € pali su na 0,04–0,05%, a za transakcije od 20.000 € na ispod 0,01%. Brzina transakcija takođe se dramatično poboljšala — 63% transfera iz EU prema Balkanu sada se završava u roku od 24 sata, u poređenju sa ranijih 6%. Izvještava RTNK.

assessments.decision_id: World Bank: Montenegro Now Has Lowest Cross-Border Payment Costs to EU After SEPA Entry
assessments.summary: Montenegro's SEPA entry on 7 October 2025 represents one of the most consequential economic integration milestones in the country's recent history. The World Bank data confirming that cross-border payment costs to the EU dropped from the highest in the Western Balkans to the lowest validates the fiscal and structural investment made in payment system modernization. The Ministry of Finance views this as a strongly positive development that will yield an estimated EUR 38 million (0.54% of GDP) in annual economic gains through lower transaction costs, improved remittance flows, enhanced trade competitiveness, and stronger foreign investment conditions. The only fiscal caution relates to reduced bank fee revenues (projected at EUR 50 million in foregone income by 2028), which will modestly reduce corporate tax receipts from the banking sector, though this is far outweighed by the broader macroeconomic benefits and alignment with EU economic convergence criteria.
assessments.executive_summary: The Ministry of Finance assesses SEPA entry as strongly positive: it delivers concrete, measurable fiscal benefits estimated at EUR 38 million annually and dramatically strengthens Montenegro's EU accession credentials. The primary concern is a modest reduction in bank fee revenues affecting corporate tax receipts, but this is vastly outweighed by economy-wide savings and improved competitiveness. The key recommendation is to accelerate the TIPS Clone instant payment system launch in 2026 to capture the full projected EUR 160 million in annual gains (2.3% of GDP).
assessments.reasoning:
From a fiscal policy perspective, SEPA integration directly supports Montenegro's macroeconomic stability by reducing friction costs in cross-border trade, which accounts for a substantial share of economic activity given the country's small, open economy heavily reliant on EU trade partners. The reduction in transfer costs from pre-SEPA levels to 0.04-0.05% for EUR 5,000 transactions and below 0.01% for EUR 20,000 transactions represents a transformative improvement in the cost structure for Montenegrin businesses engaged in EU trade.

Regarding budget impact, the effects are indirect but meaningful. Lower transaction costs reduce operating expenses for government agencies conducting cross-border payments, including debt service operations and EU-funded project disbursements. However, there is a notable fiscal trade-off: Montenegrin banks historically earned significant revenue from high cross-border transfer fees. The Central Bank of Montenegro (CBCG) projects that banks will forego approximately EUR 50 million in cumulative fee income by 2028 due to SEPA fee caps. This translates into reduced corporate tax receipts from the banking sector, estimated at EUR 4.5-5 million annually (applying Montenegro's 15% corporate tax rate to the lost revenue stream). This is a manageable fiscal cost given total budget revenues approaching EUR 3.79 billion in the 2026 draft budget.

On public debt management, SEPA integration is modestly positive. Montenegro's public debt stands near 60% of GDP, and the EUR 850 million Eurobond issued in March 2025 underscores the importance of maintaining investor confidence. SEPA membership signals institutional maturity and EU alignment, which can improve sovereign risk perception and potentially reduce borrowing spreads over time. Easier and cheaper capital flows also facilitate more efficient debt market participation.

The tax implications extend beyond the banking sector revenue loss. Greater formalization of remittance flows (as diaspora members shift from informal to formal SEPA channels due to lower costs) should broaden the tax base. The CBCG estimates EUR 13.9 million in annual savings for citizens on remittance fees alone. As more funds flow through formal banking channels, the government gains better visibility into economic activity, potentially improving VAT and income tax collection. Montenegro's large diaspora community, particularly in EU countries, will now find it dramatically cheaper to send money home, supporting household consumption and indirectly boosting VAT receipts.

For economic growth, this is unambiguously positive. The World Bank and European Commission have documented that Western Balkan economies paid up to six times more than EU counterparts for international payments. Eliminating this cost disadvantage directly improves Montenegro's competitiveness. The projected annual gain of EUR 38 million (0.54% of GDP) is significant for an economy of Montenegro's size. When combined with the upcoming TIPS Clone instant payment platform expected in mid-2026, total annual economic gains could reach EUR 160 million (2.3% of GDP), with the potential to raise per capita income by over EUR 2,500 over the next decade and increase EU income convergence from 50% to over 61%.

Foreign investment impact is strongly positive. SEPA membership removes one of the key practical barriers foreign investors cited when considering Montenegro. The ability to move funds to and from Montenegro at the same cost and speed as within the EU single market dramatically improves the investment climate. Combined with Montenegro's existing euroization (using EUR without ECB membership), SEPA effectively eliminates currency and transfer risk for EU-based investors. The US State Department's 2025 Investment Climate Statement already notes Montenegro's guarantee of profit repatriation rights; SEPA makes this operationally seamless.

Regarding EU economic criteria, SEPA membership directly advances Montenegro's position on several Maastricht-adjacent benchmarks and EU accession requirements. It demonstrates functional economic integration with the EU, strengthens the case for Montenegro's ambitious target of closing all negotiating chapters by end of 2026, and supports the broader Reform and Growth Facility agenda. The European Commission's 2025 enlargement report specifically cited payment system modernization as a positive reform indicator. With 13 of 33 chapters now provisionally closed and Chapter 32 (Financial Control) recently completed, SEPA integration reinforces the narrative that Montenegro is the most advanced Western Balkan EU candidate.

The dramatic improvement in transaction speed (EU-to-Balkans transfers completing within 24 hours rising from 6% to 63%) is particularly noteworthy from a trade finance perspective. Faster settlement reduces working capital requirements for Montenegrin importers and exporters, effectively freeing up liquidity in the economy without any fiscal cost to the government.
key_concerns: Reduced banking sector fee revenues (projected EUR 50 million in foregone income by 2028) will modestly decrease corporate tax receipts from banks, estimated at EUR 4.5-5 million annually
Banking sector profitability pressure from lost fee income could lead to higher charges on other domestic banking services, potentially offsetting some consumer benefits

Montenegro uses the euro without ECB membership, meaning SEPA integration deepens euroization dependence without gaining monetary policy tools -- any future EU payment regulation changes will apply to Montenegro without its input
The 2026 draft budget of EUR 3.79 billion leaves limited fiscal margin for shocks; while SEPA benefits are real, they should not be used to justify additional discretionary spending
Implementation risks remain for the TIPS Clone instant payment system expected mid-2026; delays could prevent Montenegro from capturing the full projected EUR 160 million in annual gains
Increased ease of cross-border transfers could facilitate capital outflows during periods of economic stress, given Montenegro lacks central bank lender-of-last-resort capacity
recommendations: Accelerate the TIPS Clone instant payment system deployment scheduled for mid-2026 to maximize the combined SEPA and real-time payment benefits projected at EUR 160 million annually (2.3% of GDP)

Establish a monitoring framework to track actual versus projected savings from SEPA integration, including metrics on remittance formalization, trade cost reduction, and FDI inflows attributable to improved payment infrastructure

Work with the CBCG to develop transitional support measures for the banking sector as fee revenues decline, potentially including incentives for banks to develop new digital payment products that generate alternative revenue streams
Leverage SEPA membership in sovereign debt marketing to improve investor perception and potentially reduce borrowing spreads on future Eurobond issuances

Integrate SEPA-related cost savings data into Montenegro's EU accession chapter negotiations, particularly for Chapters 4 (Free Movement of Capital) and 9 (Financial Services), as concrete evidence of functional economic integration

Strengthen anti-money laundering and financial intelligence capacity to manage increased cross-border payment volumes, ensuring compliance with EU AML directives and avoiding regulatory complications that could undermine SEPA benefits
Update the fiscal impact assessment in the 2026 budget to reflect reduced banking fee tax revenues and incorporate projected macroeconomic gains from SEPA into medium-term fiscal projections

counter_proposal.title: Integrated Payment Dividend Strategy
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than treating SEPA entry as a standalone achievement, the Ministry of Finance would embed the payment cost savings into a comprehensive fiscal competitiveness package. This would link SEPA benefits with targeted tax incentives for digital payment adoption, accelerated TIPS Clone deployment, and a formal mechanism to channel documented savings into productive investment rather than consumption spending.
key_changes: Create a Digital Payment Adoption Tax Credit for SMEs that transition fully to SEPA-compliant digital invoicing, worth up to EUR 500 per business annually for two years, funded by documented savings in government cross-border transaction costs

Establish a SEPA Savings Reinvestment Fund within the 2026 budget that earmarks a portion of verified macroeconomic gains from lower transaction costs toward capital investment projects, particularly digital infrastructure

Fast-track TIPS Clone deployment from mid-2026 to Q1 2026 by allocating additional resources from the NextGenPay World Bank project, capturing an estimated additional EUR 40-50 million in annual benefits from instant payments six months earlier
Introduce mandatory SEPA transaction reporting thresholds aligned with EU AML standards to improve financial transparency and broaden the tax base through better visibility of cross-border flows

Negotiate with the banking association a voluntary transition period agreement where banks gradually adjust fee structures rather than absorbing the full shock immediately, maintaining sector stability while ensuring consumer benefits are passed through
expected_benefits: Accelerated SME digitalization improving tax compliance and broadening the VAT base by an estimated EUR 5-8 million annually
Earlier capture of TIPS Clone benefits worth EUR 40-50 million annually if deployment is advanced by six months
Structured reinvestment of SEPA savings into productive capital rather than consumption, supporting long-term GDP growth
Improved financial transparency and AML compliance strengthening Montenegro's EU accession case for Chapters 4 and 9
Managed banking sector transition preserving financial stability while ensuring cost savings reach end users
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The tax credit component requires modest budget allocation (estimated EUR 2-3 million annually) easily covered by government transaction cost savings. TIPS Clone acceleration depends on World Bank project flexibility but is technically achievable given existing infrastructure. The reinvestment fund is a budgetary reallocation requiring parliamentary approval within the 2026 budget cycle. Banking sector transition agreements are voluntary and precedented in other SEPA accession countries.
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto da se pristupanje SEPA-i tretira kao samostalno postignuće, Ministarstvo finansija bi ugradilo uštede na troškovima plaćanja u sveobuhvatni paket fiskalne konkurentnosti. Time bi se SEPA benefiti povezali s ciljanim poreskim podsticajima za usvajanje digitalnih plaćanja, ubrzanim uvođenjem TIPS Clone sistema i formalnim mehanizmom za usmjeravanje dokumentovanih ušteda u produktivne investicije umjesto u potrošnju.
key_changes_mne: Uvesti poreski kredit za usvajanje digitalnih plaćanja za MSP koji u potpunosti pređu na SEPA-usklađeno digitalno fakturisanje, u iznosu do 500 EUR po preduzeću godišnje tokom dvije godine, finansiran dokumentovanim uštedama u troškovima prekograničnih transakcija vlade

Uspostaviti Fond za reinvestiranje SEPA uštedina u okviru budžeta za 2026. godinu, koji namjenski odvaja dio verifikovanih makroekonomskih dobitaka od nižih troškova transakcija za projekte kapitalnih investicija, posebno digitalnu infrastrukturu

Ubrzati uvođenje TIPS Clone sistema sa sredine 2026. na prvi kvartal 2026. godine dodjelom dodatnih sredstava iz projekta NextGenPay Svjetske banke, čime bi se šest mjeseci ranije ostvarili procijenjeni dodatni godišnji benefiti od 40–50 miliona EUR od trenutnih plaćanja

Uvesti obavezne pragove izvještavanja o SEPA transakcijama usklađene sa EU standardima za sprečavanje pranja novca, s ciljem poboljšanja finansijske transparentnosti i proširenja poreske osnovice kroz bolji uvid u prekogranične tokove

Pregovarati s bankarskom asocijacijom o sporazumu o dobrovoljnom tranzicionom periodu, u okviru kojeg bi banke postepeno prilagođavale strukturu naknada umjesto da odmah apsorbiraju puni udar, čuvajući stabilnost sektora uz istovremeno osiguravanje da koristi budu proslijeđene potrošačima
expected_benefits_mne: Ubrzana digitalizacija MSP-a koja poboljšava poresku usklađenost i proširuje PDV osnovicu za procijenjenih 5–8 miliona EUR godišnje
Ranije ostvarivanje benefita TIPS Clone sistema vrijednih 40–50 miliona EUR godišnje, ukoliko se uvođenje ubrza za šest mjeseci
Strukturirano reinvestiranje SEPA uštedina u produktivni kapital umjesto u potrošnju, što podržava dugoročan rast BDP-a
Poboljšana finansijska transparentnost i usklađenost s propisima o sprečavanju pranja novca, što jača poziciju Crne Gore u procesu pristupanja EU u okviru Poglavlja 4 i 9
Upravljana tranzicija bankarskog sektora kojom se čuva finansijska stabilnost, uz osiguravanje da uštede u troškovima stignu do krajnjih korisnika
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Komponenta poreskog kredita zahtijeva skromno budžetsko izdvajanje (procijenjeno 2–3 miliona EUR godišnje), koje lako pokrivaju uštede vlade na troškovima transakcija. Ubrzanje TIPS Clone sistema zavisi od fleksibilnosti projekta Svjetske banke, ali je tehnički ostvarivo s obzirom na postojeću infrastrukturu. Fond za reinvestiranje predstavlja budžetsku preraspodijelu koja zahtijeva parlamentarno odobrenje u okviru budžetskog ciklusa za 2026. godinu. Sporazumi o tranziciji bankarskog sektora su dobrovoljni i imaju presedan u drugim zemljama koje su pristupile SEPA-i.

assessments.summary_mne: Pristupanje Crne Gore SEPA sistemu 7. oktobra 2025. godine predstavlja jedan od najznačajnijih milestonova ekonomske integracije u novijoj istoriji ove zemlje. Podaci Svjetske banke koji potvrđuju da su troškovi prekograničnih plaćanja prema EU pali sa najvišeg na najniži nivo u Zapadnom Balkanu potvrđuju fiskalna i strukturna ulaganja u modernizaciju platnog sistema. Ministarstvo finansija ovo ocjenjuje kao izrazito pozitivan razvoj koji će donijeti procijenjene godišnje ekonomske koristi od 38 miliona EUR (0,54% BDP-a) kroz niže transakcione troškove, poboljšane tokove doznaka, veću konkurentnost u trgovini i povoljnije uslove za strana ulaganja. Jedina fiskalna napomena odnosi se na smanjenje prihoda banaka od naknada (projektovano izgubljeni prihod od 50 miliona EUR do 2028. godine), što će skromno umanjiti prihode od poreza na dobit iz bankarskog sektora, no to je daleko nadmašeno širim makroekonomskim koristima i usklađivanjem s kriterijumima ekonomske konvergencije EU.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo finansija ocjenjuje ulazak u SEPA sistem kao izrazito pozitivan: donosi konkretne, mjerljive fiskalne koristi procijenjene na 38 miliona EUR godišnje i značajno jača kredencijale Crne Gore u procesu pristupanja EU. Primarna napomena odnosi se na skromno smanjenje prihoda od bankarskih naknada koje utiče na prihode od poreza na dobit, no to je daleko nadmašeno uštedama na nivou cjelokupne ekonomije i poboljšanom konkurentnošću. Ključna preporuka jeste ubrzanje lansiranja TIPS Clone sistema trenutnih plaćanja u 2026. godini kako bi se ostvarili puni projektovani godišnji dobici od 160 miliona EUR (2,3% BDP-a).
key_concerns_mne: Smanjenje prihoda bankarskog sektora od naknada (projektovani izgubljeni prihod od 50 miliona EUR do 2028. godine) skromno će smanjiti prihode od poreza na dobit banaka, procijenjeno na 4,5–5 miliona EUR godišnje

Pritisak na profitabilnost bankarskog sektora usljed gubitka prihoda od naknada mogao bi dovesti do viših troškova za druge domaće bankarske usluge, čime bi se potencijalno poništio dio koristi za potrošače

Crna Gora koristi euro bez članstva u ECB-u, što znači da SEPA integracija produbljuje zavisnost od eurizacije bez sticanja instrumenata monetarne politike — sve buduće izmjene propisa EU o plaćanjima primjenjivaće se na Crnu Goru bez njenog učešća u procesu donošenja odluka

Nacrt budžeta za 2026. godinu od 3,79 milijardi EUR ostavlja ograničen fiskalni prostor za nepredviđene situacije; iako su koristi od SEPA-e realne, ne smiju se koristiti kao opravdanje za dodatnu diskrecionu potrošnju

Implementacioni rizici ostaju prisutni za TIPS Clone sistem trenutnih plaćanja koji se očekuje sredinom 2026. godine; kašnjenja bi mogla spriječiti Crnu Goru da ostvari puni projektovani godišnji dobitak od 160 miliona EUR
Veća lakoća prekograničnih transfera mogla bi olakšati odliv kapitala u periodima ekonomskog stresa, s obzirom na to da Crna Gora nema centralnu banku u ulozi zajmodavca u krajnjoj instanci
recommendations_mne: Ubrzati uvođenje TIPS Clone sistema trenutnih plaćanja planiranog za sredinu 2026. godine kako bi se maksimizovale kombinovane koristi od SEPA-e i plaćanja u realnom vremenu, koje se procjenjuju na 160 miliona EUR godišnje (2,3% BDP-a)

Uspostaviti okvir za praćenje stvarnih u odnosu na projektovane uštede od SEPA integracije, uključujući pokazatelje formalizacije doznaka, smanjenja troškova trgovine i priljeva stranih direktnih investicija koji se mogu pripisati unaprijeđenoj platnoj infrastrukturi

Sarađivati s CBCG na razvoju prelaznih mjera podrške za bankarski sektor kako prihodi od naknada opadaju, što potencijalno podrazumijeva podsticaje bankama za razvoj novih digitalnih platnih proizvoda koji generišu alternativne izvore prihoda
Iskoristiti članstvo u SEPA-i u marketingu državnog duga kako bi se poboljšala percepcija investitora i potencijalno smanjili kamatni rasponi na buduće emisije evroobveznica

Integrisati podatke o uštedama povezanim sa SEPA-om u pregovore o poglavljima pristupanja Crne Gore EU, posebno za Poglavlje 4 (Slobodno kretanje kapitala) i Poglavlje 9 (Finansijske usluge), kao konkretan dokaz funkcionalne ekonomske integracije

Ojačati kapacitete za sprečavanje pranja novca i finansijsko obavještavanje radi upravljanja povećanim obimom prekograničnih plaćanja, osiguravajući usklađenost s AML direktivama EU i izbjegavajući regulatorne komplikacije koje bi mogle ugroziti koristi od SEPA-e

Ažurirati procjenu fiskalnog uticaja u budžetu za 2026. godinu kako bi se odrazilo smanjenje poreskih prihoda od bankarskih naknada i ugraditi projektovane makroekonomske dobitke od SEPA-e u srednjoročne fiskalne projekcije

assessments.summary: Montenegro's SEPA entry on 7 October 2025 and the resulting collapse in cross-border payment costs represent one of the most consequential structural reforms for the Montenegrin economy in recent years. The World Bank data confirms that transfer costs for business-to-business EU transactions have fallen to 0.04-0.05% for EUR 5,000 and below 0.01% for EUR 20,000 — making Montenegro the cheapest corridor in the Western Balkans for EU payments. Combined with the dramatic improvement in transaction speed (from 6% to 63% of EU-to-Balkans transfers completing within 24 hours), this reform directly reduces trade friction, lowers costs for SMEs and exporters, enhances FDI attractiveness, and accelerates EU economic integration. The Central Bank estimates annual savings of approximately EUR 38 million (0.54% of GDP), which is a meaningful gain for a small open economy with a GDP of roughly EUR 7 billion.
assessments.executive_summary: Montenegro's SEPA integration is a strongly positive structural reform that has delivered measurable results: the lowest cross-border payment costs in the Western Balkans and dramatically faster transaction times. The Ministry's primary concern is ensuring these financial infrastructure gains translate into real productivity improvements for SMEs, export diversification, and higher-quality FDI beyond real estate. The government should now complement SEPA with targeted trade facilitation, digital payment adoption support for smaller firms, and alignment with the Common Transit Convention to maximize the competitiveness dividend.
assessments.reasoning:
From the Ministry of Economy's perspective, this development warrants a strongly positive assessment for several interconnected reasons.

First, on economic competitiveness and trade: Montenegro is a small, open, services-driven economy where cross-border transactions with the EU are fundamental to daily business operations. The EU is Montenegro's dominant trade partner, with EU exports to Montenegro totaling approximately USD 2.16 billion in 2024. Prior to SEPA, Montenegrin businesses paid up to six times more than EU counterparts for international payments, creating an invisible but significant tax on trade. The reduction to 0.04-0.05% for EUR 5,000 transfers and below 0.01% for EUR 20,000 transfers effectively eliminates this cost barrier. For SMEs conducting frequent cross-border transactions — importing inputs, paying EU-based suppliers, or receiving payments from EU customers — this translates into direct savings that improve margins and competitiveness.

Second, on FDI attraction: Montenegro received EUR 890 million in FDI in 2024, but more than half (EUR 455 million) was concentrated in real estate. SEPA integration makes Montenegro significantly more attractive for productive FDI in services, technology, and manufacturing, because investors can now move money to and from the EU at near-zero cost and with predictable timelines. The improvement in transaction speed — 63% of EU-to-Balkans transfers completing within 24 hours, up from just 6% — is particularly important for operational FDI where companies need reliable cash flow management. This complements Montenegro's November 2025 accession to the Common Transit Convention, which similarly reduces goods trade friction.

Third, on SMEs and entrepreneurship: Montenegro's SME sector has historically been disadvantaged in cross-border commerce by high payment costs and unpredictable processing times. SEPA levels this playing field significantly, enabling smaller firms to participate in EU supply chains, access EU e-commerce platforms, and engage in cross-border digital services more competitively. The Central Bank's July 2025 reform package introducing strict fee caps and abolishing discriminatory afternoon surcharges further reinforces these benefits domestically.

Fourth, on EU accession alignment: SEPA membership is a concrete step toward single market integration, directly relevant to Montenegro's goal of completing EU accession negotiations by 2026-2027. It demonstrates institutional capacity to implement complex EU-standard regulatory and technical frameworks across the entire banking sector — a signal that matters for Chapter 4 (Free Movement of Capital) and broader accession credibility.

Fifth, regarding labor market implications: While the direct labor market impact of payment system reform is indirect, lower transaction costs reduce the cost of doing business, which can support job creation particularly in trade-exposed sectors. The estimated EUR 38 million annual savings (0.54% of GDP) represents resources that can be redirected toward productive investment and employment. Additionally, cheaper remittance-like transfers benefit Montenegrin workers abroad and their families, though SEPA primarily covers business-to-business and credit transfers rather than traditional remittance channels.

The score of 9 rather than 10 reflects two residual concerns: first, the benefits accrue primarily to EU-facing transactions, while intra-Western Balkans payment costs remain high — a limitation given CEFTA trade flows. Second, the reform's benefits will be unevenly distributed, with larger firms and coastal businesses more likely to capture gains quickly, while SMEs in northern regions may lack the digital infrastructure or banking relationships to benefit immediately. The World Bank report itself focuses on B2B payments, leaving the question of whether retail and micro-enterprise payment costs have fallen proportionally.
key_concerns: Benefits are concentrated on EU-facing transactions; intra-Western Balkans payment costs remain disproportionately high, limiting CEFTA trade gains
Risk of uneven benefit distribution: larger firms and coastal businesses will capture SEPA savings faster than SMEs in underdeveloped northern regions lacking digital banking infrastructure
Over half of FDI (EUR 455 million of EUR 890 million in 2024) still flows to real estate rather than productive sectors; SEPA alone will not redirect this without complementary industrial policy
The World Bank report covers B2B payments; it remains unclear whether micro-enterprises and sole traders conducting smaller transactions see proportional cost reductions
Montenegro's banking sector must sustain SEPA compliance over time, requiring ongoing investment in IT systems, cybersecurity, and regulatory alignment that may strain smaller banks
recommendations: Launch a targeted SME digitalization program to ensure smaller firms — particularly in northern and rural regions — can access SEPA-enabled banking services and integrate electronic payments into their operations

Use the SEPA cost savings data to actively market Montenegro to productive FDI sectors (technology, business services, light manufacturing) rather than relying on the existing real estate-heavy investment profile
Negotiate with regional partners through CEFTA and the Western Balkans Common Market initiative to extend low-cost payment corridors to intra-Balkans trade, complementing the EU-facing SEPA gains

Commission a follow-up study specifically measuring SEPA impact on micro-enterprises and sole traders to identify whether additional fee caps or support measures are needed at the smallest transaction levels
Align SEPA integration benefits with the Common Transit Convention accession (November 2025) through a unified trade facilitation strategy that reduces both payment and customs costs simultaneously
Establish monitoring mechanisms through the Central Bank and Ministry of Economy to track whether SEPA savings are being passed through to end-users or absorbed by intermediaries

counter_proposal.title: SEPA-Plus: Integrated Trade and Payment Competitiveness Strategy
counter_proposal.summary: While fully endorsing SEPA integration, the Ministry of Economy would complement it with a broader competitiveness package that ensures payment cost reductions translate into measurable gains in trade volumes, productive FDI, and SME internationalization — rather than remaining a financial infrastructure achievement that primarily benefits larger, already-internationalized firms.
key_changes: Create an SME Cross-Border Trade Facilitation Fund (EUR 5 million from SEPA-related savings) providing grants for digital payment integration, e-invoicing adoption, and export readiness for firms in underdeveloped municipalities

Establish a joint Ministry of Economy and Central Bank dashboard publicly tracking SEPA impact metrics: average transaction costs by firm size, transaction volumes, new cross-border trading relationships, and regional distribution of benefits
Negotiate bilateral instant payment linkages with Serbia and Albania (Montenegro's key CEFTA partners) to extend low-cost payment benefits beyond the EU corridor

Integrate SEPA messaging into the Investment Promotion Agency's FDI marketing, with specific targeting of fintech, business process outsourcing, and digital services investors who value seamless EU payment connectivity
Require banks to offer standardized SEPA-compliant accounts with transparent fee schedules for micro-enterprises and sole traders, with Central Bank oversight to prevent fee creep
expected_benefits: Higher SME participation in EU cross-border trade within 12-18 months, particularly from northern regions currently underrepresented in export activity
Diversification of FDI away from real estate toward productive sectors that value payment infrastructure as an operational advantage
Reduced intra-Western Balkans payment costs, strengthening regional trade and CEFTA commitments
Transparent public monitoring preventing banks from absorbing SEPA savings rather than passing them to businesses
Stronger evidence base for EU accession negotiations demonstrating concrete single market integration benefits
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The financial infrastructure is already in place through SEPA. The proposed additions are primarily policy coordination, monitoring, and targeted spending measures that fall within existing institutional mandates of the Ministry of Economy, Central Bank, and Investment Promotion Agency. The SME fund could be financed from a fraction of the estimated EUR 38 million annual SEPA savings. Bilateral payment negotiations with Serbia and Albania can build on existing CEFTA cooperation frameworks and World Bank technical assistance programs already active in the region.
counter_proposal.title_mne: SEPA-Plus: Integrisana strategija konkurentnosti u trgovini i plaćanjima
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Uz punu podršku integraciji SEPA-e, Ministarstvo ekonomije bi je dopunilo širim paketom mjera za povećanje konkurentnosti, koji bi osigurao da smanjenje troškova plaćanja rezultira mjerljivim rastom obima trgovine, produktivnog SDI i internacionalizacije MSP-ova — umjesto da ostane tek infrastrukturno finansijsko dostignuće koje prvenstveno koristi većim, već internacionalizovanim preduzećima.
key_changes_mne: Uspostaviti Fond za olakšavanje prekogranične trgovine MSP-ova (5 miliona EUR iz uštedina vezanih za SEPA-u), koji će pružati grantove za integraciju digitalnih plaćanja, uvođenje e-fakturisanja i pripremu za izvoz preduzećima iz nerazvijenih opština

Osnovati zajednički dashboard Ministarstva ekonomije i Centralne banke koji javno prati pokazatelje uticaja SEPA-e: prosječne troškove transakcija po veličini preduzeća, obim transakcija, nove prekogranične poslovne odnose i regionalnu raspodjelu koristi
Pregovarati o bilateralnim vezama za instant plaćanja sa Srbijom i Albanijom (ključnim partnerima Crne Gore u okviru CEFTA-e), radi proširenja benefita nisko-trošknih plaćanja izvan EU koridora

Integrisati SEPA poruke u marketinšku strategiju Agencije za promociju investicija u oblasti SDI, s posebnim fokusiranjem na investitore iz oblasti finteha, autsorsinga poslovnih procesa i digitalnih usluga koji cijene besprijekornu EU platnu povezanost

Obavezati banke da nude standardizovane SEPA-usaglašene račune s transparentnim rasporedom naknada za mikropreduzetnike i preduzetnike pojedince, uz nadzor Centralne banke radi sprečavanja prikrivenog rasta naknada
expected_benefits_mne: Veće učešće MSP-ova u prekograničnoj trgovini s EU u roku od 12–18 mjeseci, naročito iz sjevernih regija koje su trenutno nedovoljno zastupljene u izvoznim aktivnostima
Diversifikacija SDI-a od nekretnina ka produktivnim sektorima koji platnu infrastrukturu vrednuju kao operativnu prednost
Smanjenje troškova plaćanja unutar Zapadnog Balkana, čime se jačaju regionalna trgovina i obaveze u okviru CEFTA-e
Transparentno javno praćenje koje sprečava banke da zadržavaju uštedine od SEPA-e umjesto da ih prenesu na privredu
Snažnija evidencijalna osnova za pregovore o pristupanju EU, kojom se demonstriraju konkretne koristi od integracije u jedinstveno tržište
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Finansijska infrastruktura je već uspostavljena kroz SEPA-u. Predložene dopune odnose se prevashodno na koordinaciju politika, praćenje i mjere ciljanog trošenja koje su u okvirima postojećih institucionalnih nadležnosti Ministarstva ekonomije, Centralne banke i Agencije za promociju investicija. Fond za MSP-ove mogao bi se finansirati iz dijela procijenjenih godišnjih uštedina od 38 miliona EUR nastalih zahvaljujući SEPA-i. Bilateralni pregovori o plaćanjima sa Srbijom i Albanijom mogu se nadovezati na postojeće okvire saradnje u okviru CEFTA-e i programe tehničke pomoći Svjetske banke koji su već aktivni u regionu.

assessments.summary_mne: Ulazak Crne Gore u SEPA sistem 7. oktobra 2025. godine i posljedični pad troškova prekograničnih plaćanja predstavljaju jednu od najznačajnijih strukturnih reformi za crnogorsku ekonomiju u posljednjih nekoliko godina. Podaci Svjetske banke potvrđuju da su troškovi transfera za B2B transakcije sa EU pali na 0,04–0,05% za iznos od 5.000 EUR i ispod 0,01% za iznos od 20.000 EUR — čime je Crna Gora postala najjeftiniji koridor u Zapadnom Balkanu za plaćanja prema EU. U kombinaciji sa dramatičnim poboljšanjem brzine transakcija (udio transfera iz EU ka Balkanu završenih u roku od 24 sata porastao je sa 6% na 63%), ova reforma direktno smanjuje prepreke u trgovini, snižava troškove za MSP i izvoznike, povećava atraktivnost za strane direktne investicije i ubrzava ekonomsku integraciju sa EU. Centralna banka procjenjuje godišnje uštedine od oko 38 miliona EUR (0,54% BDP-a), što predstavlja značajan dobitak za malu otvorenu ekonomiju sa BDP-om od oko 7 milijardi EUR.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Integracija Crne Gore u SEPA sistem predstavlja izrazito pozitivnu strukturnu reformu koja je dala mjerljive rezultate: najniže troškove prekograničnih plaćanja u Zapadnom Balkanu i znatno brže transakcije. Primarni prioritet Ministarstva jeste osigurati da ovi dobici u finansijskoj infrastrukturi dovedu do stvarnih poboljšanja produktivnosti MSP-a, diversifikacije izvoza i kvalitetnijih stranih direktnih investicija van sektora nekretnina. Vlada bi sada trebalo da dopuni SEPA sistem ciljanim mjerama olakšavanja trgovine, podrškom za usvajanje digitalnih plaćanja u manjim preduzećima i usklađivanjem sa Konvencijom o zajedničkom tranzitu, kako bi se u potpunosti ostvario dividenda u konkurentnosti.
key_concerns_mne: Koristi su koncentrisane na transakcije prema EU; troškovi plaćanja unutar Zapadnog Balkana ostaju nesrazmjerno visoki, što ograničava dobitke od CEFTA trgovine

Rizik od neravnomjerne raspodjele koristi: veće firme i preduzeća na primorju brže će iskoristiti SEPA uštedine nego MSP u nerazvijenim sjevernim regionima koji nemaju adekvatnu digitalnu bankarsku infrastrukturu

Više od polovine SDI (455 miliona od 890 miliona EUR u 2024. godini) i dalje odlazi u nekretnine umjesto u produktivne sektore; SEPA sama po sebi neće promijeniti ovaj trend bez komplementarne industrijske politike
Izvještaj Svjetske banke odnosi se na B2B plaćanja; nije jasno da li mikro-preduzeća i preduzetnici koji obavljaju manje transakcije ostvaruju proporcionalna smanjenja troškova

Bankarski sektor Crne Gore mora dugoročno održavati usklađenost sa SEPA standardima, što zahtijeva kontinuirana ulaganja u IT sisteme, sajber bezbjednost i regulatornu usklađenost, a to može predstavljati teret za manje banke
recommendations_mne: Pokrenuti ciljani program digitalizacije za MSP kako bi se manjim preduzećima — posebno u sjevernim i ruralnim područjima — omogućio pristup bankarskim uslugama prilagođenim SEPA sistemu i integracija elektronskih plaćanja u njihovo poslovanje

Koristiti podatke o smanjenju troškova u okviru SEPA sistema za aktivno privlačenje produktivnih stranih direktnih investicija (tehnologija, poslovne usluge, laka industrija), umjesto da se i dalje oslanjamo na postojeći profil investicija dominiranih nekretninama

Pregovarati sa regionalnim partnerima u okviru CEFTA i inicijative Zajedničkog tržišta Zapadnog Balkana o proširenju koridora plaćanja s niskim troškovima na unutarbalkansku trgovinu, kao dopunu SEPA dobitaka ostvarenih prema EU

Naručiti dopunsku studiju koja će konkretno mjeriti uticaj SEPA sistema na mikro-preduzeća i preduzetnike, kako bi se utvrdilo da li su potrebni dodatni limiti naknada ili mjere podrške na nivou najmanjih transakcija

Uskladiti koristi od SEPA integracije sa pristupanjem Konvenciji o zajedničkom tranzitu (novembar 2025) kroz jedinstvenu strategiju olakšavanja trgovine koja istovremeno smanjuje i troškove plaćanja i carinske troškove
Uspostaviti mehanizme praćenja putem Centralne banke i Ministarstva ekonomije kako bi se pratilo da li se SEPA uštedine prenose na krajnje korisnike ili ih zadržavaju posrednici

assessments.summary: Montenegro's SEPA entry and the resulting collapse in cross-border payment costs represent a moderately positive development for the education sector. The decision directly benefits Montenegrin students and researchers participating in EU-funded programs such as Erasmus+ by eliminating costly transfer fees and dramatically reducing payment processing times. It also facilitates smoother financial flows for international university partnerships, joint research projects, and procurement of educational materials from EU suppliers. However, the decision is not education-specific and does not address the structural challenges facing Montenegro's education system, including chronic underfunding, brain drain, and gaps in teacher training. Furthermore, the very same financial integration that benefits students abroad may marginally lower the friction for permanent emigration, exacerbating brain drain unless paired with deliberate retention measures.
assessments.executive_summary: Montenegro's SEPA entry is a positive but indirect benefit for education, primarily easing financial flows for Erasmus+ participants, international research collaborations, and EU-funded education programs. The top concern is that reduced financial friction may marginally accelerate brain drain among students and young researchers unless retention incentives are strengthened. The Ministry recommends coupling SEPA benefits with a dedicated student and researcher return program that leverages the same low-cost payment infrastructure to channel diaspora expertise back into Montenegrin institutions.
assessments.reasoning:
The World Bank report confirming Montenegro's dramatic reduction in cross-border payment costs following SEPA entry on 7 October 2025 has several education-relevant dimensions that merit careful analysis.

DIRECT BENEFITS FOR STUDENT MOBILITY AND ERASMUS+: Montenegro participates in Erasmus+ as a partner country, and since the program's launch in 2015, 27 projects have been approved involving Montenegrin universities. Erasmus+ grant payments flow from EU institutions to Montenegrin students and vice versa. Previously, transfer fees on a typical monthly Erasmus+ grant of 800-1,000 euros could consume a meaningful percentage of the stipend, particularly for students from lower-income families. With SEPA, these transfers now cost fractions of a cent per euro and arrive within hours rather than days. This is a tangible quality-of-life improvement for approximately 8,230 Montenegrin citizens currently studying abroad. The speed improvement from 6% of transfers completing within 24 hours to 63% is particularly relevant for students who depend on timely grant disbursements to pay rent and living expenses.

HIGHER EDUCATION AND RESEARCH FUNDING FLOWS: The University of Montenegro and private institutions increasingly participate in EU-funded research consortia, Horizon Europe projects, and bilateral academic cooperation. These programs involve frequent cross-border financial transactions for equipment procurement, conference travel reimbursements, researcher salaries, and project milestone payments. The reduction in transfer costs and processing times removes an administrative friction that previously disadvantaged Montenegrin institutions relative to EU-based partners. This makes Montenegrin universities more attractive as consortium partners, potentially increasing their access to competitive EU research funding.

EDUCATION INFRASTRUCTURE AND PROCUREMENT: Montenegro's education modernization program involves over 64 million euros in investment, including an 11 million euro EU grant and 18 million euro EIB loan for modern education infrastructure. Cross-border payment efficiency directly affects the cost and speed of procuring educational technology, laboratory equipment, and digital learning platforms from EU suppliers. Lower transaction costs mean more of the allocated budget reaches its intended purpose rather than being absorbed by banking intermediaries.

BRAIN DRAIN CONCERNS: This is the most complex dimension of the analysis. Montenegro faces severe brain drain, with estimates suggesting it could lose a quarter to half of its skilled and educated young citizens in coming decades. According to available data, 8,230 Montenegrin citizens reside abroad for educational reasons, with 43% studying in Serbia. SEPA integration removes one of the few remaining financial frictions that differentiated domestic and cross-border economic life. While this friction was never a meaningful deterrent to emigration, its removal is symbolically and practically aligned with deeper EU integration, which historically correlates with increased outward mobility from accession countries. The EU Parliament itself raised concerns in early 2025 about brain drain risks in the enlargement process.

However, SEPA also enables brain circulation mechanisms. Montenegrin researchers and professionals working in the EU can more easily maintain financial ties with Montenegrin institutions, accept part-time teaching or research contracts, and send remittances with negligible friction. This creates an opportunity for the education system to leverage diaspora expertise if appropriate programs are established.

VOCATIONAL EDUCATION AND TRAINING: Montenegro's VET system, which is being reformed under the Program for the Development of General Secondary Education 2024-2026, increasingly involves cross-border apprenticeships and partnerships with EU-based companies. SEPA integration simplifies the financial logistics of these arrangements, potentially making it easier for Montenegrin VET institutions to establish practical training partnerships with firms in Austria, Germany, and Italy, where significant Montenegrin diaspora communities exist.

LIMITATIONS: This decision is fundamentally a financial infrastructure achievement, not an education policy. It does not address any of the structural challenges in Montenegro's education system: the need for curriculum modernization, teacher professional development, quality assurance in higher education, or alignment with EU education benchmarks. Montenegro's education spending remains below EU averages, and the education system continues to produce outcomes below OECD/PISA standards. SEPA entry is a necessary but far from sufficient condition for education sector improvement.
key_concerns: SEPA's reduction of financial friction may marginally facilitate permanent emigration of students and young researchers, compounding Montenegro's already severe brain drain problem without corresponding retention mechanisms.
The decision provides no direct investment in education quality, teacher training, or institutional capacity; it is purely financial infrastructure that benefits the education sector only indirectly.

Montenegrin universities may find it easier to lose faculty to EU institutions as salary transfers become seamless, while the underlying salary gap between Montenegrin and EU academic positions remains unaddressed.
There is no evidence of a coordinated strategy between the Central Bank's SEPA implementation and the Ministry of Education to maximize educational benefits or mitigate brain drain risks.

Speed and cost improvements benefit outbound student mobility more immediately than they attract inbound academic talent to Montenegro, given the country's existing challenges in higher education quality and research infrastructure.
recommendations: Establish a SEPA-enabled Diaspora Academic Engagement Program that uses the new low-cost payment infrastructure to contract Montenegrin researchers abroad for part-time teaching, thesis supervision, and guest lectures at the University of Montenegro and other institutions.

Negotiate with banks to ensure that SEPA cost reductions are fully passed through to students and educational institutions, not absorbed as bank margin; consider regulatory guidance from the Central Bank requiring zero-fee SEPA transfers for education-related payments below 1,000 euros.

Create a dedicated Erasmus+ Support Office that leverages SEPA speed improvements to guarantee same-day grant disbursement to outbound students, removing a historical barrier to participation by lower-income students.

Develop a Brain Circulation Strategy jointly with the Ministry of Finance and Central Bank that pairs SEPA integration with funded return fellowships, portable pension contributions for returning academics, and recognition of EU-acquired qualifications.

Use SEPA infrastructure to pilot cross-border VET apprenticeship payment systems with partner institutions in Austria, Germany, and Italy, building on the 2024-2026 secondary education reform program.
Commission a study tracking the impact of SEPA on student mobility patterns and academic brain drain within 18 months of implementation to inform evidence-based policy adjustments.

counter_proposal.title: Education-Linked SEPA Dividend Program
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than treating SEPA entry as a standalone financial infrastructure achievement, the Ministry of Education would advocate for an Education-Linked SEPA Dividend Program that channels a portion of the measurable cost savings into targeted education investments. The program would mandate zero-fee SEPA transfers for all education-related transactions, establish a diaspora academic engagement platform, and create a Brain Circulation Fund financed by redirecting a fraction of the savings that banks and businesses realize from lower transaction costs.
key_changes: Mandate zero-fee SEPA transfers for verified education-related payments including tuition, grants, scholarships, and research funding below 5,000 euros.

Create a digital Diaspora Academic Platform connecting Montenegrin researchers abroad with domestic institutions for remote teaching, co-supervision, and collaborative research, with seamless SEPA-based compensation.

Establish a Brain Circulation Fund of 500,000 euros annually, financed through a micro-levy on commercial SEPA transactions above 50,000 euros, to fund return fellowships for Montenegrin academics and researchers.
Develop SEPA-integrated cross-border VET apprenticeship payment corridors with Austria, Germany, and Italy as pilot countries.
Commission an 18-month longitudinal study on the impact of SEPA on student mobility, brain drain patterns, and academic labor market dynamics in Montenegro.
expected_benefits: Direct financial relief for approximately 8,230 Montenegrin students studying abroad through eliminated transfer fees on grant and family support payments.
Increased participation of lower-income students in Erasmus+ and other EU mobility programs due to removal of financial barriers.
Structured re-engagement of Montenegro's academic diaspora through compensated remote teaching and research collaboration.
Sustainable funding mechanism for brain circulation that does not compete with existing education budget allocations.
Evidence base for future policy through longitudinal tracking of SEPA's impact on education mobility patterns.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: Moderately feasible. Zero-fee education transfers require Central Bank regulatory guidance but align with existing consumer protection trends in SEPA countries. The Diaspora Academic Platform can build on existing University of Montenegro international cooperation infrastructure. The micro-levy on high-value commercial transactions is politically sensitive but precedented in EU member states. The 500,000 euro annual Brain Circulation Fund is modest relative to Montenegro's total education investment of over 64 million euros. The main implementation risk is inter-ministerial coordination between Education, Finance, and the Central Bank.
counter_proposal.title_mne: SEPA Program Dividendi Povezan s Obrazovanjem
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto da se ulazak u SEPA tretira kao samostalno dostignuće finansijske infrastrukture, Ministarstvo prosvjete bi se zalagalo za SEPA Program Dividendi Povezan s Obrazovanjem, koji usmjerava dio mjerljivih uštedina troškova u ciljana obrazovna ulaganja. Program bi obavezao na transfere bez naknade putem SEPA-e za sve transakcije vezane za obrazovanje, uspostavio bi platformu za akademsko angažovanje dijaspore i kreirao Fond za cirkulaciju mozgova, finansiran preusmjeravanjem dijela uštedina koje banke i preduzeća ostvaruju zahvaljujući nižim troškovima transakcija.
key_changes_mne: Obavezati na SEPA transfere bez naknade za verifikovana plaćanja vezana za obrazovanje, uključujući školarinu, grantove, stipendije i finansiranje istraživanja ispod 5.000 eura.

Kreirati digitalnu Dijasporsku akademsku platformu koja povezuje crnogorske istraživače u inostranstvu s domaćim institucijama radi daljinskog predavanja, ko-mentorstva i zajedničkih istraživanja, uz besprekornu SEPA kompenzaciju.

Uspostaviti Fond za cirkulaciju mozgova u iznosu od 500.000 eura godišnje, finansiran mikro-naknadom na komercijalne SEPA transakcije iznad 50.000 eura, radi finansiranja stipendija za povratak crnogorskih akademaca i istraživača.
Razviti SEPA-integrisane koridore za plaćanje prekograničnih strukovnih pripravništava s Austrijom, Njemačkom i Italijom kao pilot-zemljama.
Naručiti longitudinalnu studiju u trajanju od 18 mjeseci o uticaju SEPA-e na mobilnost studenata, obrasce odliva mozgova i dinamiku akademskog tržišta rada u Crnoj Gori.
expected_benefits_mne: Direktno finansijsko olakšanje za oko 8.230 crnogorskih studenata koji studiraju u inostranstvu, kroz ukidanje naknada za transfer grantova i porodičnih uplata.
Povećano učešće studenata nižeg imovinskog statusa u programu Erazmus+ i drugim EU programima mobilnosti, zahvaljujući uklanjanju finansijskih prepreka.
Strukturisano ponovno angažovanje crnogorske akademske dijaspore kroz plaćena daljinska predavanja i istraživačke saradnje.
Održivi mehanizam finansiranja cirkulacije mozgova koji ne konkuriše postojećim budžetskim izdvajanjima za obrazovanje.
Evidencijska osnova za buduće politike kroz longitudinalno praćenje uticaja SEPA-e na obrasce obrazovne mobilnosti.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Umjereno izvodljivo. Transferi bez naknade u oblasti obrazovanja zahtijevaju regulatorna uputstva Centralne banke, ali su usklađeni s postojećim trendovima zaštite potrošača u SEPA zemljama. Dijasporska akademska platforma može se nadograditi na postojeću infrastrukturu međunarodne saradnje Univerziteta Crne Gore. Mikro-naknada na visoko-vrijednosne komercijalne transakcije politički je osjetljiva, ali ima presedan u državama članicama EU. Godišnji Fond za cirkulaciju mozgova od 500.000 eura skroman je u odnosu na ukupna crnogorska ulaganja u obrazovanje koja premašuju 64 miliona eura. Glavni rizik pri implementaciji je međuresorna koordinacija između Ministarstva prosvjete, Ministarstva finansija i Centralne banke.

assessments.summary_mne: Ulazak Crne Gore u SEPA zonu i posljedični pad troškova prekograničnih plaćanja predstavljaju umjereno pozitivan razvoj za sektor obrazovanja. Odluka direktno koristi crnogorskim studentima i istraživačima koji učestvuju u programima koje finansira EU, kao što je Erasmus+, eliminišući skupe naknade za transfer i drastično smanjujući vrijeme obrade plaćanja. Takođe olakšava nesmetane finansijske tokove za međunarodna partnerstva između univerziteta, zajedničke istraživačke projekte i nabavku obrazovnih materijala od dobavljača iz EU. Međutim, odluka nije specifična za obrazovanje i ne bavi se strukturnim izazovima s kojima se suočava crnogorski obrazovni sistem, uključujući hronično nedovoljno finansiranje, odliv mozgova i nedostatke u obuci nastavnika. Štaviše, upravo ta finansijska integracija koja koristi studentima u inostranstvu može marginalno smanjiti prepreke za trajnu emigraciju, čime se pogoršava odliv mozgova ukoliko se ne sprovode osmišljene mjere zadržavanja.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ulazak Crne Gore u SEPA zonu pozitivan je, ali indirektan benefit za obrazovanje, koji primarno olakšava finansijske tokove za učesnike programa Erasmus+, međunarodne istraživačke saradnje i obrazovne programe koje finansira EU. Ključna briga je da bi smanjenje finansijskih prepreka moglo marginalno ubrzati odliv mozgova među studentima i mladim istraživačima, ukoliko se ne ojačaju podsticaji za zadržavanje. Ministarstvo preporučuje da se prednosti SEPA-e upare s posebnim programom povratka studenata i istraživača, koji koristi istu infrastrukturu plaćanja niskih troškova kako bi se ekspertiza dijaspore usmjerila nazad u crnogorske institucije.
key_concerns_mne: Smanjenje finansijskih prepreka putem SEPA-e može marginalno olakšati trajnu emigraciju studenata i mladih istraživača, čime se produbljuje već ozbiljan problem odliva mozgova u Crnoj Gori, bez odgovarajućih mehanizama zadržavanja.

Odluka ne predviđa direktna ulaganja u kvalitet obrazovanja, obuku nastavnika ili institucionalne kapacitete; radi se isključivo o finansijskoj infrastrukturi koja obrazovnom sektoru koristi samo posredno.

Crnogorskim univerzitetima može postati lakše da izgube nastavno osoblje u korist institucija u EU kako transferi plata budu postajali besprijekorni, dok razlika u platama između crnogorskih i evropskih akademskih pozicija ostaje neriješena.

Ne postoje dokazi o koordiniranoj strategiji između implementacije SEPA-e od strane Centralne banke i Ministarstva prosvjete u cilju maksimalnog iskorišćavanja obrazovnih prednosti ili ublažavanja rizika od odliva mozgova.

Poboljšanja u brzini i troškovima više i neposrednije idu u prilog odlaznoj studentskoj mobilnosti nego što privlače dolazne akademske talente u Crnu Goru, s obzirom na postojeće izazove u oblasti kvaliteta visokog obrazovanja i istraživačke infrastrukture.
recommendations_mne: Uspostaviti Program akademskog angažovanja dijaspore zasnovan na SEPA-i, koji koristi novu infrastrukturu plaćanja niskih troškova za angažovanje crnogorskih istraživača u inostranstvu na pola radnog vremena radi izvođenja nastave, mentorstva pri izradi teza i gostujućih predavanja na Univerzitetu Crne Gore i drugim institucijama.

Pregovarati s bankama kako bi se osiguralo da se smanjenje troškova putem SEPA-e u potpunosti prenese na studente i obrazovne institucije, a ne zadrži kao bankarska marža; razmotriti regulatorne smjernice Centralne banke kojima se zahtijevaju SEPA transferi bez naknade za plaćanja u obrazovne svrhe ispod 1.000 eura.

Osnovati poseban Ured za podršku programu Erasmus+ koji koristi unaprjeđenja u brzini SEPA plaćanja kako bi se garantovala isplata grantova studentima koji odlaze u inostranstvo isti dan, čime se otklanja istorijska prepreka učešću studenata s nižim primanjima.

Razviti Strategiju cirkulacije mozgova u saradnji s Ministarstvom finansija i Centralnom bankom, kojom se SEPA integracija povezuje s finansiranim stipendijama za povratak, prenosivim penzijskim doprinosima za akademce koji se vraćaju i priznavanjem kvalifikacija stečenih u EU.

Koristiti SEPA infrastrukturu za pilotiranje sistema prekograničnih plaćanja za stručne prakse u okviru strukovnog obrazovanja i obuke (VET) s partnerskim institucijama u Austriji, Njemačkoj i Italiji, nadovezujući se na program reforme srednjeg obrazovanja za period 2024–2026.

Naručiti studiju koja će pratiti uticaj SEPA-e na obrasce studentske mobilnosti i akademski odliv mozgova u roku od 18 mjeseci od implementacije, kako bi se informisala prilagođavanja politika zasnovana na dokazima.

assessments.decision_id: World Bank: Montenegro Now Has Lowest Cross-Border Payment Costs to EU After SEPA Entry
assessments.summary: Montenegro's SEPA entry and the resulting dramatic reduction in cross-border payment costs is a positive development for the health sector, though its impact is indirect. The healthcare system relies heavily on pharmaceutical and medical device imports from the EU (approximately $139 million in pharmaceutical imports alone), and lower transaction costs and faster settlement times reduce friction in procurement. The WHO has identified out-of-pocket medicine spending as the primary driver of catastrophic health expenditure in Montenegro, affecting 9% of households and 33% of the poorest quintile. While SEPA-driven savings on transaction fees are modest relative to total pharmaceutical costs, they contribute to a broader ecosystem of reduced trade barriers that can incrementally lower input costs for the health system, ease cross-border patient treatment payments, and reduce the cost of remittances from Montenegro's significant health worker diaspora.
assessments.executive_summary: SEPA entry is a positive but indirect benefit for Montenegro's health sector. The Ministry's top concern is that reduced payment costs alone will not address the structural crisis of catastrophic out-of-pocket medicine spending affecting 9% of households. The key recommendation is to channel transaction cost savings into expanding pharmaceutical coverage and reforming medicine copayment policy as recommended by WHO.
assessments.reasoning:
The Ministry of Health assesses Montenegro's SEPA entry and the World Bank's confirmation of lowest cross-border payment costs as a moderately positive development with several channels of impact on the health sector.

First, pharmaceutical and medical device procurement. Montenegro imports virtually all of its pharmaceuticals and a large share of medical devices from EU member states and Switzerland. According to Observatory of Economic Complexity data, pharmaceutical imports totaled approximately $139 million, with major suppliers including Serbia, Switzerland, Hungary, the Netherlands, and Slovenia. The Health Insurance Fund (Fond za zdravstveno osiguranje) processes payments to EU-based pharmaceutical distributors and manufacturers regularly. Reducing transaction costs from what were the highest in the Western Balkans to the lowest (0.04-0.05% on 5,000 euro transactions, below 0.01% on 20,000 euro transactions) removes a layer of cost from these procurement chains. For large-volume hospital and institutional purchases, which routinely involve transactions well above 20,000 euros, the savings are meaningful in aggregate even if small per transaction.

Second, the dramatic improvement in transaction speed (from 6% of EU-to-Balkans transfers completing within 24 hours to 63%) is significant for healthcare supply chain management. Faster payment settlement reduces the risk of supply disruptions for essential medicines, especially time-sensitive biologics and vaccines that require rapid procurement cycles. The Clinical Center of Montenegro (Klinicki centar Crne Gore) and the network of public hospitals depend on reliable, timely procurement from EU suppliers.

Third, cross-border patient treatment. Montenegro routinely sends patients abroad for specialized treatments unavailable domestically, particularly complex oncological, cardiac, and pediatric interventions, often to hospitals in Serbia, Italy, Germany, and Turkey. The Health Insurance Fund covers these costs and must transfer payments internationally. Lower transaction costs and faster processing directly benefit this critical function and reduce administrative overhead.

Fourth, health workforce and remittances. Montenegro experiences significant brain drain in the health sector, with doctors and nurses emigrating to EU countries, particularly Germany, Austria, and Slovenia. Remittances constitute approximately 11% of GDP (843.62 million euros in 2024 according to IOM data). Cheaper and faster transfers from health workers abroad to their families in Montenegro ease financial pressure on households and indirectly reduce reliance on out-of-pocket health spending.

However, the Ministry must be clear-eyed about the limitations. The WHO's April 2025 report on financial protection in Montenegro's health system found that 9% of households face catastrophic health spending, driven primarily by out-of-pocket costs for outpatient medicines. Among the poorest quintile, this figure reaches 33%. The structural problems identified by WHO, including weaknesses in medicine coverage design and supply, internal reference pricing that creates avoidable copayments, administrative barriers limiting exemption uptake, and the absence of income-linked copayment caps, will not be solved by lower transaction fees. The SEPA savings, while welcome, are marginal relative to the scale of the pharmaceutical affordability crisis.

Furthermore, the benefits of SEPA for the health sector depend on whether savings in procurement costs are actually passed through to the health system and ultimately to patients, rather than being absorbed by intermediaries. There is no automatic mechanism ensuring that lower banking fees translate into lower medicine prices or expanded coverage.

From an EU integration perspective, SEPA membership is a concrete step toward alignment with EU financial infrastructure, which is complementary to the health acquis under Chapter 28 (Consumer and Health Protection). Integration into EU payment systems facilitates future participation in EU health programs, cross-border healthcare arrangements under Directive 2011/24/EU, and joint procurement mechanisms for vaccines and essential medicines. This is a building block, not a solution in itself, but it is a necessary one.
key_concerns: Transaction cost savings in pharmaceutical procurement may be absorbed by intermediaries (distributors, banks) rather than passed through to the Health Insurance Fund or patients, with no mechanism to ensure pass-through.

SEPA entry does not address the structural pharmaceutical affordability crisis identified by WHO, where 9% of households and 33% of the poorest quintile face catastrophic out-of-pocket medicine costs.
Lower transfer costs could marginally accelerate health workforce emigration by making it easier for diaspora health workers to support families remotely, reducing pressure to return.
The government's framing emphasizes macroeconomic benefits without articulating a specific strategy for channeling savings into health system improvements.

Faster and cheaper payments alone do not address the regulatory and supply-chain barriers that limit medicine availability in Montenegro, including issues with CALIMS registration processes and limited pharmacy networks in rural areas.
recommendations: Mandate that the Health Insurance Fund track and report savings from reduced cross-border transaction costs on pharmaceutical and medical device procurement, ensuring transparency on whether savings reach the health budget.
Use SEPA integration as a platform to negotiate better payment terms with EU-based pharmaceutical suppliers, leveraging faster settlement to secure early-payment discounts on essential medicines.

Direct a portion of documented transaction cost savings toward expanding outpatient medicine coverage for chronic conditions, in line with WHO recommendations on reducing catastrophic health spending.
Accelerate CALIMS alignment with EU pharmaceutical regulatory standards (EMA procedures) to complement financial integration with regulatory integration, reducing barriers to medicine availability.
Develop a cross-border healthcare payment framework that leverages SEPA infrastructure for smoother processing of patient treatment abroad reimbursements, reducing administrative delays.

Implement the WHO-recommended reforms: replace percentage copayments with fixed amounts, introduce income-linked copayment caps, and expand pharmacy network choice, using any fiscal space created by SEPA savings as partial funding.

counter_proposal.title: Health-Linked SEPA Savings Framework
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than treating SEPA savings as diffuse macroeconomic gains, the Ministry of Health would establish a targeted mechanism to capture and reinvest transaction cost savings from health-related cross-border payments into pharmaceutical affordability reforms. This would pair the financial integration achievement with concrete health system improvements, creating a direct link between EU integration milestones and patient-level benefits.
key_changes: Establish a Health Transaction Savings Account within the Health Insurance Fund that captures documented savings from reduced cross-border payment costs on pharmaceutical and medical device imports.
Use accumulated savings to fund WHO-recommended copayment reforms, starting with fixed copayments replacing percentage-based ones for the 20 most commonly used chronic disease medicines.
Create a SEPA-enabled direct procurement channel between the Health Insurance Fund and EU pharmaceutical wholesalers, bypassing expensive intermediary layers.
Develop a digital cross-border patient treatment payment portal using SEPA infrastructure, reducing processing time for treatment-abroad reimbursements from weeks to days.
expected_benefits: Transparent tracking of health procurement savings estimated at 200,000-500,000 euros annually based on current pharmaceutical import volumes and the new transaction cost rates.
Direct reduction in copayment burden for chronic disease patients, targeting the most vulnerable 33% of poorest households identified by WHO.
Faster reimbursement for patients treated abroad, reducing financial stress on families during medical emergencies.
Demonstration of concrete EU integration benefits in a sector that directly affects citizen wellbeing, strengthening public support for the accession process.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The proposal requires administrative rather than legislative changes. The Health Insurance Fund already processes cross-border payments and can track savings with minimal additional infrastructure. The WHO copayment reforms have already been designed and costed. SEPA infrastructure is already operational. The main challenge is inter-ministerial coordination between the Ministry of Health and the Ministry of Finance to ring-fence savings rather than absorbing them into general revenue.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir SEPA Uštedina Vezan za Zdravstvo
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto da se SEPA uštedine tretiraju kao difuzni makroekonomski dobici, Ministarstvo zdravlja bi uspostavilo ciljani mehanizam za prikupljanje i reinvestiranje uštedina na troškovima transakcija od platnih transfera u zdravstvu koji prelaze granice, u reforme dostupnosti lijekova. Time bi se finansijsko integraciono dostignuće spojilo s konkretnim poboljšanjima zdravstvenog sistema, stvarajući direktnu vezu između prekretnica EU integracije i koristi na nivou pacijenta.
key_changes_mne: Uspostavljanje Računa uštedina zdravstvenih transakcija u okviru Fonda zdravstvenog osiguranja koji evidentira dokumentovane uštedine nastale smanjenjem troškova prekograničnih plaćanja za uvoz lijekova i medicinskih sredstava.

Korišćenje akumuliranih uštedina za finansiranje reformi participacije koje preporučuje SZO, počevši od uvođenja fiksnih participacija koje zamjenjuju procentualne za 20 najčešće korišćenih lijekova za hronične bolesti.
Uspostavljanje direktnog kanala nabavke putem SEPA-e između Fonda zdravstvenog osiguranja i EU veletrgovaca lijekovima, zaobilazeći skupe posredničke slojeve.
Razvoj digitalnog portala za prekogranična plaćanja liječenja pacijenata korišćenjem SEPA infrastrukture, smanjujući vrijeme obrade refundacija za liječenje u inostranstvu s nedjelja na dane.
expected_benefits_mne: Transparentno praćenje uštedina u nabavci u zdravstvu procijenjenih na 200.000–500.000 eura godišnje, na osnovu trenutnih obima uvoza lijekova i novih stopa troškova transakcija.
Direktno smanjenje tereta participacije za pacijente s hroničnim bolestima, s fokusom na najranjijih 33% najsiromašnijih domaćinstava koje je identifikovao SZO.
Brža refundacija za pacijente liječene u inostranstvu, smanjujući finansijski pritisak na porodice u toku medicinskih hitnih slučajeva.
Demonstracija konkretnih koristi od EU integracije u sektoru koji direktno utiče na dobrobit građana, jačajući podršku javnosti procesu pristupanja.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Prijedlog zahtijeva administrativne, a ne zakonodavne promjene. Fond zdravstvenog osiguranja već obrađuje prekogranična plaćanja i može pratiti uštedine uz minimalnu dodatnu infrastrukturu. Reforme participacije SZO su već osmišljene i finansijski procijenjene. SEPA infrastruktura je već operativna. Glavni izazov je međuresorna koordinacija između Ministarstva zdravlja i Ministarstva finansija kako bi se uštedine izdvojile, a ne apsorbovale u opšte prihode.

assessments.summary_mne: Ulazak Crne Gore u SEPA i posljedično dramatično smanjenje troškova prekograničnih plaćanja predstavlja pozitivan razvoj za zdravstveni sektor, iako je njegov uticaj indirektan. Zdravstveni sistem uveliko se oslanja na uvoz farmaceutskih i medicinskih proizvoda iz EU (oko 139 miliona dolara samo u uvozu lijekova), a niži troškovi transakcija i brži rokovi namire smanjuju trenje u procesu nabavke. SZO je identifikovao izdatke za lijekove iz džepa kao primarni pokretač katastrofalnih zdravstvenih troškova u Crnoj Gori, koji pogađaju 9% domaćinstava i 33% najsiromašnijeg kvintila. Iako su uštedine na naknadama za transakcije, posredstvom SEPA-e, skromne u odnosu na ukupne troškove farmaceutskih proizvoda, one doprinose širem ekosistemu smanjenja trgovinskih barijera koji može postepeno sniziti ulazne troškove zdravstvenog sistema, olakšati plaćanja pri liječenju pacijenata u inostranstvu i smanjiti troškove doznaka dijaspore zdravstvenih radnika iz Crne Gore.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ulazak u SEPA predstavlja pozitivnu, ali indirektnu korist za zdravstveni sektor Crne Gore. Ključna briga Ministarstva je da samo smanjeni troškovi plaćanja neće riješiti strukturnu krizu katastrofalnih izdataka za lijekove iz sopstvenog džepa, koja pogađa 9% domaćinstava. Ključna preporuka je da se uštedine na troškovima transakcija usmjere ka proširenju pokrivenosti lijekovima i reformi politike participacije za lijekove, u skladu s preporukama SZO.
key_concerns_mne: Uštedine na troškovima transakcija u farmaceutskoj nabavci mogu prisvojiti posrednici (distributeri, banke), a ne prenjeti ih Fondu zdravstvenog osiguranja ili pacijentima, bez ikakvog mehanizma koji bi osigurao njihov prenos.

Ulazak u SEPA ne rješava strukturnu krizu pristupačnosti lijekova koju je identifikovala SZO, gdje 9% domaćinstava i 33% najsiromašnijeg kvintila suočava se s katastrofalnim troškovima lijekova iz sopstvenog džepa.
Niži troškovi transfera mogli bi marginalno ubrzati emigraciju zdravstvenih radnika, olakšavajući dijaspori zdravstvenih radnika da izdržavaju porodice na daljinu i smanjujući pritisak za povratak.
U okviru koji vlada prezentuje naglašavaju se makroekonomske koristi, bez jasno definisane strategije za usmjeravanje uštedina u unapređenje zdravstvenog sistema.

Brža i jeftinija plaćanja sama po sebi ne rješavaju regulatorne barijere i barijere u lancu snabdijevanja koje ograničavaju dostupnost lijekova u Crnoj Gori, uključujući probleme s procesima registracije u CALIMS-u i ograničenim mrežama apoteka u ruralnim područjima.
recommendations_mne: Obavezati Fond zdravstvenog osiguranja da prati i izvještava o uštedinama ostvarenim smanjenjem troškova prekograničnih transakcija u nabavci farmaceutskih i medicinskih proizvoda, osiguravajući transparentnost u pogledu toga da li uštedine dospijevaju do zdravstvenog budžeta.

Iskoristiti integraciju u SEPA kao platformu za pregovaranje o povoljnijim uslovima plaćanja s farmaceutskim dobavljačima sa sjedištem u EU, koristeći brže namire kao polugu za obezbjeđivanje popusta za prijevremeno plaćanje esencijalnih lijekova.

Dio dokumentovanih uštedina na troškovima transakcija usmjeriti ka proširenju pokrivenosti ambulantnih lijekova za hronična oboljenja, u skladu s preporukama SZO o smanjenju katastrofalnih zdravstvenih rashoda.

Ubrzati usklađivanje CALIMS-a s regulatornim standardima EU za farmaceutske proizvode (procedure EMA), kako bi se finansijska integracija dopunila regulatornom integracijom i smanjile barijere za dostupnost lijekova.

Razviti okvir za prekogranična plaćanja u zdravstvenoj zaštiti koji koristi SEPA infrastrukturu radi glatkijeg procesuiranja refundacija troškova liječenja pacijenata u inostranstvu, smanjujući administrativna kašnjenja.

Implementirati reforme koje preporučuje SZO: zamijeniti procentualne participacije fiksnim iznosima, uvesti gornje granice participacija vezane za prihode i proširiti izbor mreže apoteka, koristeći eventualni fiskalni prostor ostvaren uštedinama iz SEPA-e kao djelimičan izvor finansiranja.

assessments.decision_id: World Bank: Montenegro Now Has Lowest Cross-Border Payment Costs to EU After SEPA Entry
assessments.summary: Montenegro's SEPA entry and dramatically reduced cross-border payment costs are primarily a financial integration achievement with limited direct environmental impact. However, the decision carries meaningful indirect benefits for the environmental sector: it facilitates the flow of EU pre-accession and green finance funds, reduces transaction costs for importing environmental technologies and equipment from EU suppliers, and eases cross-border payments critical to the estimated EUR 1.4 billion in Chapter 27 environmental investments required by 2035. The decision is a welcome enabler but does not substitute for the substantive environmental policy reforms and infrastructure investments Montenegro urgently needs.
assessments.executive_summary: This financial integration measure is modestly positive for the environment sector, primarily by reducing friction in EU fund disbursements and environmental technology procurement. The Ministry's key concern is that payment efficiency gains must be channeled toward Montenegro's massive environmental infrastructure gap (EUR 1.4 billion for Chapter 27 compliance), and we recommend the government use SEPA-enabled cost savings to strengthen the Eco Fund and accelerate waste and water investments.
assessments.reasoning:
The SEPA entry decision is fundamentally a financial sector reform, not an environmental measure. Its relevance to the Ministry of Environment is indirect but real, operating through several channels.

First, EU fund absorption. Montenegro's Chapter 27 compliance requires an estimated EUR 1.4 billion in environmental investments by 2035, spanning waste management, wastewater treatment, air quality, and biodiversity protection. A significant portion of this financing comes through EU pre-accession instruments (IPA), the Western Balkans Investment Framework (WBIF), and institutions like the EIB and EBRD. For example, the EIB signed a EUR 22.5 million grant in March 2025 for water supply and wastewater systems in Kolasin, Rozaje, and Mojkovac. Reduced transaction costs and faster settlement times (from days to hours) will modestly improve the efficiency of disbursing and receiving these funds.

Second, environmental technology procurement. Montenegro imports nearly all of its environmental infrastructure equipment (wastewater treatment plants, air quality monitoring systems, waste sorting and recycling machinery, renewable energy components) from EU member states. The reduction in cross-border payment costs from the previous high levels to 0.04-0.05% for EUR 5,000 transactions and below 0.01% for EUR 20,000 transactions directly lowers procurement costs for municipalities and environmental agencies purchasing this equipment. While the percentage savings are small in absolute terms, across the scale of Chapter 27 investments they accumulate.

Third, green finance facilitation. The EBRD and EU have launched the SME Go Green programme in Montenegro, providing loans and grant incentives for green investments.
